professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: We've been discussing how museums acquired many of their collections during the colonial era. Recently, several major museums have faced demands to return artifacts to their countries of origin. Some argue these objects belong to the cultures that created them, while others believe large museums preserve them better and share them with a global audience. Should museums be required to return such artifacts? Why or why not?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I believe artifacts should be returned to their countries of origin. These objects carry deep cultural and spiritual meaning for the communities that created them, and displaying them abroad strips away that context. Returning them also acknowledges a history of unequal power and lets people reconnect with their own heritage.

students.name: Andrew
students.text: In my opinion, major museums are often the better guardians. When I visited one last year, I saw fragile artifacts preserved with a care that smaller institutions simply cannot match. Keeping diverse collections together also lets millions of visitors encounter cultures they might otherwise never see.

professor.name: Dr. Diaz
professor.text: Consider the smartphone in your pocket. Many manufacturers design products with a limited lifespan — batteries that cannot be replaced, software that stops updating — a strategy known as planned obsolescence. Some argue it is a legitimate practice that funds continuous innovation, while others see it as an unethical trick that wastes both resources and consumers' money. Is planned obsolescence ever ethically justifiable?

students.name: Kelly
students.text: I think planned obsolescence is difficult to justify ethically. Deliberately designing a product to fail pushes consumers to buy replacements they do not really need, which drains their money and fills landfills with electronic waste. Companies should compete on quality, not on how quickly their products break.

students.name: Paul
students.text: Planned obsolescence is not automatically unethical. Shorter product cycles fund the research that gives us faster, safer, more efficient devices each year. As long as companies are honest about a product's expected lifespan, consumers can make an informed choice and pay for durability if they truly want it.

professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: We've been discussing brand identity this semester. Lately, more companies are taking public positions on social and political issues, from climate change to voting rights. Some marketers argue this builds trust and loyalty with customers who share those values, while others say it alienates half the market and should be avoided entirely. Do you think brands should take public stands on controversial issues?

students.name: Andrew
students.text: I believe brands should take a stand when an issue genuinely connects to their values. Consumers, especially younger ones, increasingly expect companies to stand for something beyond profit. A well-chosen position can deepen loyalty and set a brand apart in a crowded, competitive market.

students.name: Claire
students.text: In my opinion, most companies should stay neutral. Marketing exists to sell products, not to divide the audience, and taking sides on a controversial issue inevitably angers a large segment of customers. A brand that wades into politics also risks looking opportunistic rather than sincere.

professor.name: Dr. Achebe
professor.text: Recently, many schools have started paying students small cash rewards for good grades or perfect attendance. Supporters point to studies showing that such incentives boost short-term effort, especially among struggling students. Critics counter that external rewards can erode a child's natural curiosity, so learning stops the moment the payments do. Do rewards like these help or ultimately harm students' motivation to learn?

students.name: Andrew
students.text: Paying students for grades usually backfires in the long run. Once the cash appears, children start working for the reward instead of out of genuine interest, and that curiosity is hard to rebuild. The moment the money stops, so does the effort it was meant to encourage.

students.name: Kelly
students.text: For students who have already given up, a concrete reward can be exactly the push they need to re-engage. Motivation is not fixed — success in one subject often sparks real interest later. Used carefully and then phased out, incentives can jump-start learning rather than replace it.
